elements.sent-2411-001: The Oja depth (simplicial volume depth) is one of the classical statistical techniques for measuring the central tendency of data in multivariate space.
elements.sent-2411-002: Despite the widespread emergence of object data like images, texts, matrices or graphs, a well-developed and suitable version of Oja depth for object data is lacking.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address this shortcoming, in this study we propose a novel measure of statistical depth, the metric Oja depth applicable to any object data.
elements.sent-2411-004: Then, we develop two competing strategies for optimizing metric depth functions, i.e., finding the deepest objects with respect to them.
elements.sent-2411-005: Finally, we compare the performance of the metric Oja depth with three other depth functions (half-space, lens, and spatial) in diverse data scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-006: Nowadays, data is being generated in high volumes, at high speeds, and with great diversity.
elements.sent-2411-007: In fact, it can be stated that with the advent of big data, we have entered a new phase of data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-008: A key aspect of modern data analysis is that we no longer deal exclusively with data existing in Euclidean spaces.
elements.sent-2411-009: Instead, data are currently taking on more complex formats, such as images, graphs, matrices, etc., all of which reside in non-Euclidean spaces and are collectively known as object data.
elements.sent-2411-010: Methods targeted towards object data, accordingly known as object data analysis or metric statistics \citep{dubey2024metric , are currently bountiful in statistical literature, see, e.g., \cite{dubey2022modeling, virta2022sliced, zhang2023dimension, zhu2023geodesic .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: As with any data, in order to achieve a better understanding of object data or to implement both simple and advanced machine learning algorithms on object data, it is essential to take some initial steps.
elements.sent-2411-012: These involve data cleaning and performing exploratory data analysis on the raw data.
elements.sent-2411-013: Exploratory data analysis can take various forms: calculating descriptive statistics, dimension reduction, detecting outliers etc. are all examples of exploratory data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, despite being straightforward and well-studied in the context of Euclidean data, these tasks can be surprisingly difficult in the context of object data, due to the lack of even basic mathematical operations such as addition.
elements.sent-2411-015: In this work, we focus on one of the most fundamental exploratory statistical tasks, location/mean/average estimation, in the context of object data.
elements.sent-2411-016: As our methodological tool of choice, we use depth functions, one of the lesser-known yet powerful and data-driven tools in exploratory data analysis, which we review next.
elements.sent-2411-017: In statistical analysis, measuring the extent to which an observation is centralized or outlying within the data distribution plays a fundamental role.
elements.sent-2411-018: Subsequently, various statistical measures have been developed for this purpose and the history of this concept can be traced back to the early 1900s, to the concept of Mahalanobis distance.
elements.sent-2411-019: In fact, this distance serves as a classic method for quantifying how outlying a point is.
elements.sent-2411-020: In contrast to Mahalanobis distance and similar quantities, depth functions operate precisely in the opposite direction and measure the centrality, or depth, of a point with respect to a distribution (outlyingness and centrality are two contrasting concepts).
elements.sent-2411-021: In mathematical terms, for a point $x$ and a probability distribution $P$, a depth function $D$ assigns to $x$ a non-negative value $D(x, P)$ which quantifies the centrality of $x$ with respect to the probability distribution $P$.
elements.sent-2411-022: A higher depth value indicates that $x$ is located closer to the center of the data, while a lower depth indicates that this point is more outlying within the distribution.
elements.sent-2411-023: Going beyond the previous heuristic explanation, an ideal depth function is typically required to satisfy a certain collection of properties, see~\cite{zuo2000general .}
elements.sent-2411-024: The foundation for depth functions was originally introduced in 1975 by Tukey who proposed his seminal half-space depth for multivariate data to rank observations and reveal features of the underlying data distribution~\citep{tukey1975mathematic .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Apart from the half-space depth, numerous depth functions for data in Euclidean space have been proposed.
elements.sent-2411-026: For instance, the convex hull peeling depth (onion depth) \citep{barnett1976ordering, eddy1981graphics or Simplicial volume depth (Oja depth) \citep{oja1983descriptive and several others, see Table \ref{tab1 and the comprehensive addressing in \cite{mosler2022choosing .}}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \textwidth{!}{ \begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|c|} Author Year Depth function Has a metric version Mahalanobis \citep{mahalanobis1936generalized} 1936 Mahalanobis depth (distance) No Tukey \citep{tukey1975mathematic} 1975 Half-space/location/Tukey depth Yes \citep{dai2023tukey} Barnett, Eddy \citep{barnett1976ordering, eddy1981graphics} 1976, 1981 Convex hull peeling /onion depth No Liu; Zuo \& Serfling \citep{liu1992data, zuo2000general} 1992, 2000 Projection depth No Liu \citep{Liu1990} 1990 Simplicial depth No Oja, Zuo \& Serfling \citep{oja1983descriptive} 1983, 2000 Simplicial volume depth/Oja depth Yes, in the current paper Koshevoy \& Mosler \citep{koshevoy1997zonoid} 1997 Zonoid depth No Vardi \& Zhang, Serfling \citep{vardi2000multivariate, serfling2002depth} 2000, 2002 Spatial depth Yes \citep{virta2023spatial} Liu \& Modarres \citep{LiuModarres2011} 2011 Lens depth Yes \citep{cholaquidis2023weighted, geenens2023statistical} Yang \& Modarres \citep{yang2018beta} 2018 $\beta$-skeleton depths No \end{tabular} } \caption{List of depth functions and whether a metric version of them exists.} \label{tab1}
elements.sent-2411-027: In recent years, object/metric versions of various depth functions (see the final column of Table \ref{tab1 ) have been proposed to analyze samples of object data living in an arbitary metric space $(\mathcal{X , d)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: That is, these new depth functions are such that given an object $X \in \mathcal{X}$ and a distribution $P$ taking values in $\mathcal{X}$, the depth $D(X; P)$ describes how central the object $X$ is w.r.t.
elements.sent-2411-029: Two key properties of these extensions is that (a) they depend on the data only through the metric $d$, making them applicable to any form of object data, regardless in which specific metric space they live, and (b) if $(\mathcal{X , d)$ is chosen to be a Euclidean space, then the original Euclidean version of the depth is recovered, showing that these metric depth functions are indeed ``true'' generalization of their classical counterparts.}
elements.sent-2411-030: A metric version of the lens depth was developed in \cite{cholaquidis2023weighted, geenens2023statistical , the metric half-space depth was proposed in \cite{dai2023tukey and the metric spatial depth in \cite{virta2023spatial .}}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Most of the depth functions in Table \ref{tab1 , in particular all three that have object versions, are robust \citep{maronna2019robust , meaning that their performance is not skewed by the presence of possible outliers.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The same is not true for location estimation procedures in general.
elements.sent-2411-033: For example, the Fr{\'e chet mean \citep{frechet1948elements which is a generalization of the concept of average and a classical estimator of location for object data, is not robust, but is instead affected greatly by data outliers.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Despite the popularity of Fr{\'e chet mean and similar tools, in this work we concentrate solely on robust methods.}
elements.sent-2411-035: This is because object data is very diverse and recognizing any possible outliers in them can, as such, be very difficult.
elements.sent-2411-036: Hence, to ensure that our analyses stay reliable, we find it imperative to develop robust methods, which automatically protect us against outliers regardless of their exact type and whether we recognize them or not.
elements.sent-2411-037: As stated earlier, our focus in this work is on the robust location estimation of object data using depth functions.
elements.sent-2411-038: The main contributions of this work in this regard are as follows.
elements.sent-2411-039: As with the metric generalizations of the other depth functions, the metric Oja depth is applicable to any object data and reverts back to the original Oja depth in Euclidean spaces.
elements.sent-2411-040: Like its competitors, it is also robust.
elements.sent-2411-041: Interestingly, the generalization process of Oja depth actually leads to several metric versions of it, indexed by a integer-valued dimension parameter $p$, see Section \ref{sec:new_depth for details, where we also derive several theoretical properties of the metric Oja depth that help interpreting it.}
elements.sent-2411-042: That is, given a sample $X_1, \ldots, X_n$ of object data whose empirical distribution we denote by $P_n$, we aim to find the object $X \in \mathcal{X}$ which has the maximal depth value $D(X; P_n)$.
elements.sent-2411-043: This deepest point serves as the analogue of sample mean for object data and has obvious uses in data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-044: Despite the fundamental nature of this problem, in the earlier works on metric depths it has been considered only by \cite{dai2023tukey , and even they restricted to finding the optimum among the sample points $X_1, \ldots, X_n$.}
elements.sent-2411-045: As a more comprehensive approach, we propose using a genetic algorithm \citep{katoch2021review along with a coordinate representation of the object data to estimate the deepest out-of-sample object.}
elements.sent-2411-046: We demonstrate this with the metric Oja depth, but the proposed algorithm applies equally to any other metric depth function.
elements.sent-2411-047: As one of our main questions of interest, we investigate how much the in-sample estimation can be improved by using the computationally expensive genetic algorithms to achieve out-of-sample estimation.
elements.sent-2411-048: In both simulation scenarios involving correlation matrices and points on hyperspheres, metric Oja depth performs best, with metric half-space depth showing the weakest performance but requiring less processing time.
elements.sent-2411-049: Applying the genetic algorithm to metric Oja depth further improved its performance.
elements.sent-2411-050: We note that a prominent underlying theme in this work is the use of computationally intensive techniques to perform statistical inference for object data: e.g., since obtaining the null distribution for testing the equality of the deepest points of two distributions is not feasible, we use a permutation test to approximate this distribution in the absence of any distributional assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-051: Similarly, since depth functions are computationally complex and since a generic metric space does not carry enough structure for implementing standard optimization, we represent the objects as approximate coordinates and use genetic algorithms for heuristic optimization.
elements.sent-2411-052: As shown in Table \ref{tab1 , three depth functions for object data have been developed earlier in the literature, which will be briefly introduced in Section~\ref{sec:review .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: In Section \ref{sec:new_depth , we provide a detailed explanation of a new metric depth function (two versions of that) developed in this research, outlining its features within theoretical framework.}
elements.sent-2411-054: In Section \ref{sec:simu , we evaluate and compare it with three other depth functions, using two simulation scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-055: We also apply a genetic algorithm to optimize out-of-sample error, and all experimental results are presented in this section.
elements.sent-2411-056: In Section \ref{sec:real_data , we further tested our metric depth function on real dataset to assess its performance in statistical inference, relying on computational techniques.Finally, future recommendations and conclusions are compiled in Section \ref{sec:discussion .}}
elements.section-2411-002: Review of existing metric depth functions
elements.sent-2411-057: We next review the three existing depth functions for object data proposed in literature.
elements.sent-2411-058: We let $(\Omega, \mathcal{F , \mathbb{P )$ be a probability space and take $(\mathcal{X , d)$ to be a complete and separable metric space where our data (called hereafter ``objects'') resides.}}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Further, we let $P$ be a probability measure defined on the Borel sets of $\mathcal{X}$.
elements.sent-2411-060: Given a fixed, non-random object $x \in \mathcal{X}$, the metric depth functions answer the question ``how central is the object $x$ with respect to the distribution $P$?''.
elements.sent-2411-061: To tackle this quesition, \cite{cholaquidis2023weighted, geenens2023statistical proposed metric lens depth, defined as \begin{equation}D_L(x) \equiv D_L(x; P) := P \left( d(X_1, X_2) > \max \left\{ d( X_1, x), d(X_2, x) \right\} \right),\end{equation} where $X_1, X_2$ are independent random variables with the distribution $P$.}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}D_L(x) \equiv D_L(x; P) := P \left( d(X_1, X_2) > \max \left\{ d( X_1, x), d(X_2, x) \right\} \right),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-062: We note that while the notation $D_L(x; P)$ is more transparent in the sense that it makes explicit the reference distribution $P$, we still use $D_L(x)$ in the sequel for conciseness, leaving $P$ implicit (but always clear from the context).
elements.sent-2411-063: Drawing an analogy from Euclidean statistics, $D_L(x)$ essentially gives the probability that the side corresponding to $X_1, X_2$ is the longest in a ``triangle'' drawn using the objects $x, X_1, X_2$.
elements.sent-2411-064: Intuitively, the probability is small (large) if $x$ is located far away from (close to) the bulk of the distribution $P$, making $D_L$ behave as expected from a depth function.
elements.sent-2411-065: The infimum is taken over pairs of objects $x_1, x_2$ and every such pair divides the space $\mathcal{H}$ in two subsets (objects closer to $x_1$ than $x_2$ and vice versa).
elements.sent-2411-066: The depth $D_H(x)$ is defined to be the smallest possible probability mass of a subset produced in this way and containing the object $x$.
elements.sent-2411-067: As with the metric lens depth, it is easy to see that, for an outlying object $x$, it is easy to find a subset which contains $x$ but has very little $P$-probability mass, making $D_H(x)$ small.
elements.sent-2411-068: A third metric depth was proposed in \cite{virta2023spatial , who defined the metric spatial depth to be \begin{equation}\textwidth{!}{$D_S(x) = 1 - \frac{1}{2} E \left[ I(d(X_1, x) \neq 0, d(X_2, x) \neq 0) \left\{ \frac{ d^2(X_1, \mu) + d^2(X_2, \mu) - d^2(X_1, X_2) }{d(X_1, \mu) d(X_2, \mu)} \right\} \right],$}\end{equation} where $X_1, X_2 \sim P$ are independent.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\textwidth{!}{$D_S(x) = 1 - \frac{1}{2} E \left[ I(d(X_1, x) \neq 0, d(X_2, x) \neq 0) \left\{ \frac{ d^2(X_1, \mu) + d^2(X_2, \mu) - d^2(X_1, X_2) }{d(X_1, \mu) d(X_2, \mu)} \right\} \right],$}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-069: The interpretation of $D_S(x)$ is trickier than for the previous two depths, but it essentially measures how likely $x$ and two random objects $X_1, X_2$ are to yield equality in the triangle inequality.
elements.sent-2411-070: The depth takes values in $[0, 2]$ and the value $0$ is reached if and only if the probability of equality is 1 and $x$ is never in the middle of the other two objects, whereas to reach the value $2$, $x$ must always reside between the two other objects.
elements.sent-2411-071: More details on interpreting the metric spatial are given in \cite{virta2023spatial .}
elements.sent-2411-072: All previous metric depth functions revert to their classical counterparts when $(\mathcal{X , d)$ is taken to be an Euclidean space.}
elements.sent-2411-073: They also share some key properties, which we summarize next.
elements.sent-2411-074: All three depths vanish at infinity meaning that, for objects far enough away from the bulk of the distribution $P$, the depth values approach zero.
elements.sent-2411-075: All three satisfy specific forms of continuity (small changes in $x$ entail small changes in the depth).
elements.sent-2411-076: Both $D_L$ and $D_H$ are invariant to data transformations that preserve the ordering of distances.
elements.sent-2411-077: The depths $D_H$ and $D_S$ are robust, the former in the sense of having a high breakdown point and the latter in the sense of having a bounded influence function.
elements.sent-2411-078: For these, and some other properties of the three depth functions, we refer the reader to the papers \cite{cholaquidis2023weighted, geenens2023statistical, dai2023tukey, virta2023spatial .}
elements.sent-2411-079: Let $X_1, \ldots, X_n$ denote a random sample of objects from the distribution $P$.
elements.sent-2411-080: Each of the three depth functions admits a natural sample counterpart where the reference distribution is taken to be $P_n$ the empirical distribution of the sample.
elements.sent-2411-081: In the sequel, we notate these sample versions as $D_{L, n , D_{H, n , D_{S, n}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Thus, for example, the sample metric lens depth of $x$ is \begin{equation}D_{L, n}(x) \equiv D_L(x; P_n) := \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i = 1}^n \sum_{j = 1}^n I \left( d(X_i, X_j) > \max \left\{ d( X_i, x), d(X_j, x) \right\} \right).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}D_{L, n}(x) \equiv D_L(x; P_n) := \frac{1}{n^2} \sum_{i = 1}^n \sum_{j = 1}^n I \left( d(X_i, X_j) > \max \left\{ d( X_i, x), d(X_j, x) \right\} \right).\end{equation}

elements.section-2411-003: A new depth for object data
elements.sent-2411-086: Before defining our novel depth concept, we first take a moment to motivate it.
elements.sent-2411-087: For any three objects $x_1, x_2, x_3 \in \mathcal{X}$, we use the notation $L(x_1, x_2, x_3)$ to denote the event that $d(x_1, x_3) = d(x_1, x_2) + d(x_2, x_3)$.
elements.sent-2411-088: To gain some intuition on this event, consider the following thought experiment: Assume that ``transforming'' an object $x \in \mathcal{X}$ to another object $y \in \mathcal{X}$ incurs a cost of $d(x, y)$, i.e., the farther apart (or, more different) the two objects are, the more costly the transformation.
elements.sent-2411-089: The triangle inequality can then be phrased as saying that a direct transformation from one object to another can never be more costly than going through a third object.
elements.sent-2411-090: Whereas, the event $L(x_1, x_2, x_3)$ says that when transforming $x_1$ to $x_3$, such a ``detour'' through $x_2$ is actually free of cost.
elements.sent-2411-091: i.e., one may first transform $x_1$ to $x_2$ and then transform $x_2$ to $x_3$, with the same total cost as going directly from $x_1$ to $x_3$.
elements.sent-2411-092: For this to be possible, it is clear that $x_2$ has to reside (in some sense) ``between'' $x_1$ and $x_3$.
elements.sent-2411-093: Following the above intuition, to simplify the exposition, we use in the following phrases such as ``$x_2$ lies in between $x_1$ and $x_3$'' to mean that the event $L(x_1, x_2, x_3)$ holds.
elements.sent-2411-094: We also introduce the union event \begin{equation}U(x_1, x_2, x_3) := L(x_1, x_2, x_3) \cup L(x_2, x_3, x_1) \cup L(x_3, x_1, x_2).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}U(x_1, x_2, x_3) := L(x_1, x_2, x_3) \cup L(x_2, x_3, x_1) \cup L(x_3, x_1, x_2).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-095: Thus, $U(x_1, x_2, x_3)$ means that at least one of $x_1, x_2, x_3$ is located in between the remaining two objects.
elements.sent-2411-096: To put the previous ideas of in-betweenness to use, we next define two matrices which are intimately connected to them.
elements.sent-2411-097: Let first $x_0, x_1, x_2, x_3 \in \mathcal{X}$ be arbitrary objects.
elements.sent-2411-098: We denote by $B_3(x_0, x_1, x_2, x_3)$ the $3 \times 3$ matrix whose $(k, \ell)$-element equals \begin{equation}\frac{1}{2} \left\{ d^2(x_0, x_k) + d^2(x_0, x_\ell) - d^2(x_k, x_\ell) \right\}.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\frac{1}{2} \left\{ d^2(x_0, x_k) + d^2(x_0, x_\ell) - d^2(x_k, x_\ell) \right\}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-099: Moreover, we let $B_2(x_0, x_1, x_2)$ denote the $2 \times 2$ top left principal sub-matrix of $B_3(x_0, x_1, x_2, x_3)$.
elements.sent-2411-100: Our next result shows that the determinants of these two matrices contain interesting information on the relations between the four objects.
elements.sent-2411-101: The notation $| \cdot |$ in the result denotes the determinant.
elements.sent-2411-102: We have \begin{equation}\left| B_2(x_0, x_1, x_2) \right| \geq 0,\end{equation} where an equality is reached if and only if the event $U(x_0, x_1, x_2)$ holds.
elements.sent-2411-103: We have \begin{equation}\label{eq:case_p_3} \left| B_3(x_0, x_1, x_2, x_3) \right| \geq - 4 d^2(x_0, x_1) d^2(x_0, x_2) d^2(x_0, x_3).\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\label{eq:case_p_3} \left| B_3(x_0, x_1, x_2, x_3) \right| \geq - 4 d^2(x_0, x_1) d^2(x_0, x_2) d^2(x_0, x_3).\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-104: If equality is reached in \eqref{eq:case_p_3 , then at least one of the events $L(x_1, x_0, x_2)$, $L(x_2, x_0, x_3)$, $L(x_3, x_0, x_1)$ holds.}
elements.sent-2411-105: We now build measures of depth using the two matrices.
elements.sent-2411-106: Let $X_1, X_2, X_3 \sim P$ be independent random objects and consider, for a fixed object $x \in \mathcal{X}$, the quantity \begin{equation}G_3(x) = E \left[ \left\{ | B_3(x, X_1, X_2, X_3) | + 4 d^2(x, X_1) d^2(x, X_2) d^2(x, X_3) \right\} ^{1/2} \right].\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-107: By Theorem \ref{theo:lower_bound_1 , taking the square root is well-defined (we will prove that the expectation exists shortly).}

elements.sent-2411-233: In this section, we aim to evaluate the performance of the proposed depth functions using real data.
elements.sent-2411-234: The secondary aim of this experiment is to demonstrate how the depth functions, whose analytically complex form prevents theoretical inferential results, can still be used for statistical inference via relying on computational techniques.
elements.sent-2411-235: For this purpose, the Canadian Weather data were utilized, see \cite{ramsay2005functional .}
elements.sent-2411-236: This data set contains the average daily temperatures of 35 Canadian provinces over a year and, as is standard, we smoothed them into periodic functional data $X_i(t)$, $i = 1, \ldots , 35$, $t = 0, \ldots, 365$, using a 100-element Fourier basis in the R-package fda \citep{Rfda .}
elements.sent-2411-237: The 35 stations each belong to one of four geographic regions: Arctic, Continental, Pacific, and Atlantic.
elements.sent-2411-238: Based on the geographic locations of these regions, we hypothesize that the temperatures in the coastal areas, i.e., the Atlantic and Pacific, should be similar, while the temperatures in the northern (Arctic) and central southern (Continental) regions of Canada should be colder and differ from the previous two.
elements.sent-2411-239: This is visually confirmed in Figure \ref{canadacmp (left), where the Atlantic and Pacific regions are generally warmer throughout the year, while the Arctic and Continental regions are colder.}
elements.sent-2411-240: We thus divide the $n = 35$ stations into two groups — the eastern and western coastal areas, and the northern and central southern areas — and our research question is: Is there a significant difference in the typical temperature curves between these two groups?.
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{deepestcmp2.pdf} \caption{Left panel: The yearly average temperatures for $n = 35$ Canadian weather stations, divided into two groups according to geographical location. Right panel: the deepest in-sample curves in each group, as determined by MOD3 with $L_2$-distance as the metric.} \label{canadacmp}
elements.sent-2411-241: We next study this question by treating the curves as objects in a metric space $(\mathcal{X , d)$ where $d$ is the $L_p$-distance for some value of $p \in [1, \infty]$, implemented in R in the package fda.usc \citep{Rfdausc .}}
elements.sent-2411-242: According to Figure \ref{canadacmp (right), which shows the deepest in-sample curves of these two groups as determined by MOD3 with $L_2$-distance as the metric, it appears that there is indeed a difference in their yearly behaviours.}
elements.sent-2411-243: However, to confirm this, we next conduct a permutation test for determining whether the difference in the deepest curves in the two groups is statistically significant.
elements.sent-2411-244: Letting $f_1, f_2 \in \mathcal{X}$ denote the deepest in-sample curves of the two groups, we use as our test statistic the $L_p$-distance between them, $t = d(f_1, f_2)$.
elements.sent-2411-245: To simulate the distribution of $t$ under the null hypothesis (i.e., assuming no difference between the two groups), we randomly permute the group labels of the $n = 35$ curves and compute the test statistic value.
elements.sent-2411-246: This reshuffling was done a total of 5000 times, yielding $t^*_1, \ldots, t^*_{5000}$, using which the $p$-value of the test is computed as $\#\{ t \leq t^*_i \}/5000$.
elements.sent-2411-247: The resulting $p$-values for the five different depths and $L_p$-metrics with $p = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 10, 20, \infty$, are shown in Figure \ref{barrevorg (left).}
elements.sent-2411-248: These metrics measure distinct forms of difference between the curves (i.e., the higher $p$ is, the more the metric $L_p$ focuses on local properties), but all of them agree that there is a difference between the groups at the significance level $0.05$.
elements.sent-2411-249: Similarly, no quantitative differences are observed between the five depths, although MLD and MHD appear the least stable with respect to the change of $p$ (assuming that the null hypothesis is indeed false).
elements.sent-2411-250: The most consistent performance across $p$ is given by MOD3.

elements.sent-2411-263: This study introduced two new versions of the Oja depth in non-Euclidean spaces and compared their performance with three established metric depth functions.
elements.sent-2411-264: Initially, we compared the performance of these five metric estimators using two simulated datasets.
elements.sent-2411-265: Under these two simulation scenarios, MOD2 and MOD3 (the two new proposed depth functions) respectively achieved the best performance, although both were more time-consuming than the others.
elements.sent-2411-266: Moreover, the weakest performance was observed with the MHD.
elements.sent-2411-267: Among the remaining functions, only MSD demonstrated relatively good performance after MOD2 and MOD3, and was also the fastest, potentially making it a useful alternative for practical applications.
elements.sent-2411-268: Next, for the first time a metaheuristic approach (Genetic Algorithm) was employed to evaluate the introduced estimators on an unseen sample.
elements.sent-2411-269: To save time, we only selected the MOD2 and used PCA (Principal Component Analysis) for dimensionality reduction.
elements.sent-2411-270: With the genetic algorithm, we observed a substantial improvement in the selected estimator's performance across all sample sizes, from 10 to 60, compared to its performance without genetic algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-271: However, a notable downside is that genetic algorithm incurs a significant computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-272: Finally, we used a real data (Canadian weather dataset) to compare the metric depth functions' effectiveness in a statistical two-sample location hypothesis test.
elements.sent-2411-273: Initially, we examined the original data, divided into two geographically distinct groups, and used five metric depth functions (for different $p$ in \(L_{p}\) norm) to find the distance between the two deepest objects in each group.
elements.sent-2411-274: Among all methods, MOD3 consistently produced the most stable results across p-values, while MLD and MHD were the least stable.
elements.sent-2411-275: For further evaluation, we intentionally misassigned a subset of data points between groups and repeated the permutation test.
elements.sent-2411-276: In this case, MOD2 was the only one to produce incorrect results, while the others remained accurate.
elements.sent-2411-277: However, when the rate of misassignments increased, MHD became the only method to maintain correct results.
elements.sent-2411-278: In conclusion, by following the recommendations we provide, this study can be further improved in the future.
elements.sent-2411-279: As mentioned, despite the promising performance of the two new metric depths presented here, both functions involve a significant time cost.

elements.sent-2411-281: Additionally, to evaluate and compare the methods more clearly, the relative form of estimation error could be used instead of the absolute one.
